MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Summary Adjudication, In The Alternative, On Plaintiff S Complaint
Off Calendar Jan-16-2024 Continued To Apr-15-24 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Jan-05-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for leave to file a cross-complaint is granted.

Plaintiff James Rogers alleges he suffered injuries while riding a bicycle at the intersection of 37th Avenue and Taraval Street in San Francisco. Plaintiff alleges defective crosswalk striping caused his rear wheel to slip out from under him. He brought suit against the City and County of San Francisco ("City"), NTK Construction, Inc., and Asphalt Impressions, Inc. NTK signed a contract with the City to perform roadwork at the intersection, which included clauses for defense and indemnification to the City. (Memorandum, 2:1-3:6.) The City alleges that it tendered a defense and indemnification to NTK on October 7, 2022, which NTK has refused to accept. (Id.) The City now brings this motion for leave to file a cross-complaint against NTK.

A party who fails to plead a cause of action may ask the court for leave to file a cross-complaint at any time during the action. (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 426.50.) The court shall grant the motion unless the opposing party can demonstrate that the party which failed to plead the cause of action acted in bad faith. (Id.) Section 426.50 is liberally construed to avoid forfeiture of causes of action. (Silver Organizations Ltd. v. Frank (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 94, 99.) Factors such as oversight, inadvertence, neglect, mistake, etc. are insufficient grounds to deny the motion unless accompanied by bad faith. (Id.)

Here, the interests of justice weigh in favor of granting leave to file the cross-complaint. The issues in the cross-complaint are linked with the underlying facts in the complaint and allowing the cross complaint would conserve judicial resources. The City has not offered a satisfactory explanation why it brought its motion less than three months before trial, but NTK has failed to demonstrate that the City has acted in bad faith by waiting to file a cross-complaint. NTK has long been a party to this case and it has had notice of the City's tender for over a year. Nor has NTK established undue prejudice. The record does not show that NTK won't be able to mount an adequate defense to the cross-complaint.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
